FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data; N=7,614 participants with high-quality structural MRI at Year 2 (2018-2020, ages 10-13)

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media use measured as hours per day; Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ) measuring addiction; both are specific smartphone/SM variables

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports inferential statistical tests: generalized additive mixed models (GAMM) with vertexwise analysis (FEMA), region-of-interest (ROI) analysis with multiple comparisons correction (FDR), adjusted regression models

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports β coefficients with 95% confidence intervals and p-values in Table 2 and Figure 1; standardized beta coefficients reported for cortical thickness associations

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD neuroimaging data examining association between social media and brain morphology

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in NeuroImage, 2026; not a preprint, conference abstract, or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Social media is the independent variable, not dependent variable. Outcomes are brain morphological measures (cortical thickness, surface area, volume), not demographics predicting SM use. Analysis includes non-demographic outcomes.
notes: Strong paper with large sample, high-quality neuroimaging, and sophisticated statistical methods (FEMA vertexwise analysis)

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study longitudinal data (Years 2-4); N=8,292 participants; mean age 12.0 years at Year 2 (2018-2020)

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media time measured in hours per day at Year 2; specific SM variable with validated measurement

FT_IC3.evidence: Generalized structural equation modeling (GSEM) with direct and indirect effects; logistic regression with binomial distribution; mediation analysis with delta method for standard errors; p<.001 reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports B (regression coefficients) with 95% CI and p-values: B=0.14 (95% CI 0.08-0.20, p<.001) for direct effect; mediation effect B=0.009 (p=.002); proportion mediated 5.97% (95% CI 1.27%-10.67%, p=.013)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original longitudinal empirical analysis of ABCD data examining prospective association between social media time and nicotine experimentation with mediation pathway

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in American Journal on Addictions, 2026; not a duplicate or preprint

FT_EC7_reason: Includes mediation analysis with psychological mediator (smoking expectancies), not just demographic predictors. Even though longitudinal, the inclusion of a psychological/behavioral mediator makes FT-EC7 not applicable. Paper also reports smartphone/SM as IV with non-SM outcome.
notes: Longitudinal mediation study; applies E3 (latent construct/pathway extractable from SEM model)

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data from Year 5 (2021-2023); N=11,530 adolescents aged 13-16 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Dating app use (lifetime use: yes/no); qualifies under criterion as online dating/hookup app variable (app-based platform)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multivariable linear regression analyses with cluster-robust standard errors; adjusted models; reports test statistics and p-values

FT_IC4.evidence: Standardized beta coefficient [ß] 0.42; 95% confidence interval [CI] 0.08-0.76; p=0.017 reported in adjusted models; also reports unadjusted estimates

FT_IC5.evidence: Original cross-sectional empirical analysis of ABCD data examining association between dating app use and depression symptoms

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in BMC Research Notes, 2026; not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional analysis (ignores directionality per FT-EC7 rule c). Dating app use is the independent variable, depression symptoms is the dependent variable. Paper does not fit FT-EC7 pattern of demographics→SM predicting future SM use.
notes: App-based platform use qualifies as SM variable. Very small prevalence (0.9% reported ever using); dating app use treated as binary exposure variable.
